title: The Ackermann Inquisition
description: Let there be ordained a most pernicious oracle, denoted PSI, defined upon two non-negative integers by this thrice-cursed law. Shouldst the FIRST argument be naught, the oracle returneth the second argument increased by unity. Shouldst the second argument be naught yet the first be positive, the oracle deferreth to ITSELF, with the first argument diminished by one and the second restored to unity. And in every other case — O reader, take heed and tremble — the oracle invoketh itself TWICE, nesting one summons within the other: the first argument diminished by one, and the second argument being the oracle's OWN verdict upon the selfsame first argument together with a second diminished by one. Given two integers m and n (space-separated upon one line), declare the value PSI(m, n).

examples.explanation: PSI(2,2) beggeth PSI(1, PSI(2,1)); and PSI(2,1) beggeth PSI(1, PSI(2,0)); and PSI(2,0) deferreth to PSI(1,1), which unspooleth — through PSI(0, PSI(1,0)) and thence PSI(0,2) — unto 3; whereupon PSI(2,1) becometh PSI(1,3) = 5, and at the last PSI(2,2) becometh PSI(1,5) = 7. Seven, then, though the road thither be a torment.
By the same nested descent, multiplied unto exhaustion, PSI(3,3) resolveth — after a recursion that would unman a lesser scribe — to the value 61. Trust it; verify it if thou darest.
